title: Managing pig slurry on a smallholding
subtitle: Storage, safety, and spreading rules for a small-scale outdoor pig system.
excerpt: Pig slurry is a valuable fertiliser and a regulated waste product. Spreading it on the wrong day, too close to a watercourse, or without observing closed periods triggers Environment Agency enforcement. A small outdoor pig unit generates more liquid waste per animal than sheep or poultry; understanding the rules before the pigs arrive prevents serious problems later.
sourceNotes: Environment Agency, Nitrate Vulnerable Zones and slurry storage guidance, gov.uk. DEFRA, Environmental Permitting Regulations for farm waste, gov.uk. Farming Advisory Service, pig slurry spreading guidance.

glossaryTerms.definition: A mandatory winter period when slurry and organic manure cannot be applied to land. In England, the closed period for spreading slurry on arable land runs from 15 October to 31 January; on grassland from 15 October to 15 January. Within Nitrate Vulnerable Zones (NVZ), restrictions are stricter.

content.text: An outdoor pig system on deep bedding or a hard-standing area produces a mix of faeces, urine, and bedding (pig muck) that is relatively easy to manage. A fully slatted indoor system produces liquid slurry that requires more planning. Most smallholders run outdoor or semi-outdoor systems; this tutorial focuses on that context.

applies whether you are in a Nitrate Vulnerable Zone or not, though NVZ rules are more restrictive. Check your holding's NVZ status using the Environment Agency's online checker (search 'is my land in an NVZ' on gov.uk). Spreading on waterlogged ground, within 10 metres of a watercourse, or on a slope over 12° on soil near a watercourse is not permitted regardless of season.

Rotate outdoor pigs through paddocks on a rest cycle to stop the land becoming a constant source of runoff. A paddock used for pigs needs at least 6 months' rest before grazing livestock again. Annual cropping resets the nutrient balance. Keep pigs off slopes above drainage ditches. One heavy rain on a heavily dunged paddock can trigger an Environment Agency inspection.

Composting pig muck before spreading reduces odour, improves nitrogen availability, and kills most pathogens. Stack the muck at least 10 metres from any watercourse, in a covered bay or under a tarpaulin during prolonged rain. Turn the heap every 3 to 4 weeks to maintain aerobic decomposition. Pig muck that is heavily wet and anaerobic (smells strongly of ammonia) provides less plant nutrition and is more unpleasant to spread.

If you are in a Nitrate Vulnerable Zone, you must keep spreading records: date, area spread, method, volume or weight applied. These records must be retained for 5 years. Even outside an NVZ, a simple spreadsheet of spreading dates and paddocks provides the evidence you need if there is ever a pollution complaint to the Environment Agency.
